title: National Register of Historic Places listings in Pipestone County, Minnesota
rows: 1891 vestige of the abandoned town of North Sioux Falls , Minnesota , relocated circa 1893 as the first in a row of Sioux Quartzite buildings on Jasper 's main street
Burlington Cedar Rapids and Northern Depot
400 North Hiawatha Avenue 44°00′14″N 96°19′05″W / 44.003874°N 96.317986°W / 44.003874 ; -96.317986 ( Burlington Cedar Rapids and Northern Depot )
1890 railway station symbolizing the importance of railroads in southwestern Minnesota 's development
1888 hotel listed for its architectural prominence and importance to Pipestone 's early commerce . Also a contributing property to the Pipestone Commercial Historic District
Off Hiawatha Avenue 44°00′48″N 96°19′30″W / 44.013333°N 96.325°W / 44.013333 ; -96.325 ( Cannomok ' e-Pipestone National Monument )
Primary source of pipestone quarried by Plains Indians from prehistory to present for ceremonial pipes and other sacred or artistic items . Now a National Park unit
Hospital clinic built circa 1913 , noted for its unusual use of Sioux Quartzite on a Stick style design
Holman Street and Sherman Avenue 43°54′30″N 96°22′07″W / 43.908413°N 96.368562°W / 43.908413 ; -96.368562 ( Ihlen Mercantile Company )
1892 home of Ihlen 's first business , also noted as southwestern Minnesota 's most intact frame commercial building
Five-section school in service 1911-2001 , illustrating Jasper 's distinctive use of Sioux Quartzite across multiple decades and architectural styles as well as its evolving educational priorities
Along Main Street between Second Avenue NW/SW and Second Avenue NE/SE 44°00′02″N 96°19′03″W / 44.000502°N 96.317621°W / 44.000502 ; -96.317621 ( Pipestone Commercial Historic District )
Two-block district featuring Minnesota 's greatest concentration of Sioux Quartzite buildings , with 22 contributing properties mostly dating to the 1890s
1900 courthouse , significant as the county 's seat of government and most elaborate architecture and use of Sioux Quartzite
Off North Hiawatha Avenue 44°01′15″N 96°19′11″W / 44.020745°N 96.319832°W / 44.020745 ; -96.319832 ( Pipestone Indian School Superintendent 's House )
1907 staff housing , a rare vestige in Minnesota of federal Native American boarding schools such as Pipestone Indian School , which operated 1892-1953
Third Street , S.E . and South Hiawatha Avenue 43°59′55″N 96°19′02″W / 43.998609°N 96.317308°W / 43.998609 ; -96.317308 ( Pipestone Public Library )
1904 Carnegie library also significant for its architecture , designed by Joseph Schwartz in Sioux Quartzite
1920 water tower , one of the first to be built with concrete . Designer L.P. Wolff pioneered the method in 1918 for the nearly identical Brainerd Water Tower in Brainerd , Minnesota
Unusual stone bungalow built circa 1905 , juxtaposing a common design with an atypical construction material
County Road 54 over Split Rock Creek 43°53′31″N 96°22′02″W / 43.891914°N 96.367218°W / 43.891914 ; -96.367218 ( Split Rock Bridge )
Quartzite arch bridge built 1937-38 , noted for its aesthetic simplicity and 50-foot ( 15 m ) length - the longest stone arch span on a Minnesota highway
One of Jasper 's earliest and most intact quartzite buildings , constructed in 1894

description: This table lists properties in Pipestone County, Minnesota, that are included in the National Register of Historic Places. It provides details such as the name of the property, the date it was listed, its location, the city or town it is situated in, and a brief description of its historical significance. The table serves as a resource for understanding the cultural and historical landmarks in the region.
